past_medical_history: Hypertension, diagnosed 10 years ago, well controlled
Obstructive sleep apnea, uses CPAP nightly
Remote history of ankle fracture from recreational basketball, age 32
family_history: Father died suddenly at age 49 while jogging, autopsy reported as 'enlarged heart'
Paternal uncle had a defibrillator placed at age 52 for 'heart rhythm problems'
Mother alive at age 78 with hypertension and type 2 diabetes
Brother, age 52, reports occasional palpitations but has not been evaluated
No known family history of epilepsy or neurological disease

social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife and two teenage children in a suburban home
social_history.exercise: Physically active, coaches and participates in basketball drills 4-5 days per week. Notes that both episodes occurred during or shortly after vigorous activity

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert, oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Glasgow Coma Scale 15. Speech fluent with normal rate, rhythm, and prosody. No dysarthria or aphasia. Attention intact, able to perform serial 7s without error. Recall 3/3 objects at 5 minutes. No confusion. Patient is calm and conversant, asking detailed questions about why he was referred to neurology. No post-ictal features whatsoever at time of examination, which is 45 minutes after most recent event.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN II: Visual fields full to confrontation bilaterally. Pupils equal, round, reactive 4mm to 2mm bilaterally. Fundoscopic exam normal, no papilledema or hypertensive retinopathy. CN III/IV/VI: Extraocular movements intact, no nystagmus. CN V: Sensation intact to light touch in V1-V3 bilaterally. Masseter and temporalis strength symmetric. CN VII: Face symmetric at rest and with activation, normal forehead wrinkling. CN VIII: Hearing intact to finger rub bilaterally. CN IX/X: Palate elevates symmetrically, gag intact. CN XI: Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius 5/5 bilaterally. CN XII: Tongue protrudes midline without fasciculations or atrophy.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk normal and symmetric throughout. Tone normal in all four extremities, no rigidity, spasticity, or paratonia. Strength 5/5 throughout: deltoids, biceps, triceps, wrist extensors and flexors, grip, finger abductors, hip flexors, knee extensors and flexors, ankle dorsiflexors and plantarflexors bilaterally. No pronator drift. No myoclonus.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch, pinprick, temperature, proprioception, and vibration sense intact and symmetric throughout all extremities and trunk. No dermatomal or peripheral nerve distribution sensory loss.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally, patellar 2+ bilaterally, Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor (downgoing) bilaterally. No clonus.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing accurate bilaterally without dysmetria or intention tremor. Heel-to-shin testing smooth bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements intact and symmetric.
neurological_exam.gait: Steady, normal stride length and base. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative. Able to walk on heels and toes without difficulty.
neurological_exam.additional: No tongue laceration or evidence of oral trauma. No urinary incontinence reported during either episode. Small bruise on right hip from fall during second episode. No needle marks, no signs of substance use. Cardiac exam notable for regular rate and rhythm, a grade II/VI systolic murmur heard best at the left sternal border that increases with Valsalva maneuver, and a prominent S4 gallop.

patient.chief_complaint: Two episodes of loss of consciousness with shaking, referred from outside facility as 'new-onset seizures'
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Darius Coleman is a 55-year-old African American man, a high school basketball coach, who presents to the emergency department after being transferred from an outside urgent care facility with a referral diagnosis of 'new-onset seizure disorder.' His wife and an assistant coach accompanied him and provide detailed collateral history. The first episode occurred 10 days ago during an after-school basketball practice. The patient was actively demonstrating defensive drills to his players, running up and down the court, when he suddenly collapsed without any warning. Multiple witnesses, including his assistant coach, report that he fell 'like a sack of bricks' and then exhibited approximately 10-15 seconds of bilateral upper extremity jerking movements. He was reportedly pale and diaphoretic during the event. He regained consciousness within approximately 30 seconds of falling and was oriented and conversant almost immediately, though he felt fatigued and mildly nauseated. He went home early but did not seek medical attention, attributing the episode to dehydration and overexertion. The second episode occurred two days ago, again during vigorous physical activity. He was playing in a recreational basketball game at the local YMCA when, midway through the second half, he abruptly lost consciousness while running downcourt. His wife, who was watching from the bleachers, witnessed him collapse and saw his arms and legs jerk briefly for what she estimates was 5-10 seconds. He was pale and unresponsive, and another player checked his pulse and reported it felt 'very fast and irregular.' He regained consciousness quickly, within 30-45 seconds, and was again immediately oriented without any confusion, though he complained of palpitations and a sensation of his heart 'racing' for about a minute after waking. EMS was called and by the time they arrived his heart rate was 78 bpm with normal sinus rhythm on the field monitor. He was taken to the local urgent care where a physician assistant noted a 'normal' ECG and diagnosed him with 'new-onset seizures, likely exertional,' prescribing levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily and referring him to neurology. The patient has taken only two doses of levetiracetam. He denies any aura, visual changes, olfactory hallucinations, deja vu, or epigastric rising sensation before either event. He denies tongue biting, urinary incontinence, or prolonged confusion after either episode. He reports no recent head trauma prior to the episodes, no fever, no weight loss, and no chest pain. He does endorse mild exertional dyspnea that has been gradually worsening over the past 4-5 months, which he attributed to 'getting older and being out of shape.' He also notes occasional brief episodes of palpitations over the past year, lasting seconds to a few minutes, that resolve spontaneously.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 10 Hz alpha rhythm, well-formed, symmetric, and reactive to eye opening bilaterally
background.anterior_rhythms: Low-to-medium amplitude beta activity, symmetric, no focal slowing
background.sleep_features: Stage II sleep captured with vertex waves and sleep spindles, normal sleep architecture

artifacts.description: Intermittent low-amplitude muscle artifact, does not obscure underlying cerebral activity
ECG artifact identified, does not interfere with interpretation. Occasional premature ventricular complexes noted on ECG channel.

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Performed for 3 minutes. Symmetric, diffuse slowing during hyperventilation with prompt return to baseline. No epileptiform discharges provoked. No focal abnormalities.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Performed at flash frequencies 1-30 Hz. Normal symmetric photic driving response at 12-18 Hz. No photoparoxysmal response. No epileptiform discharges.

eeg.impression: Normal awake and asleep EEG. No epileptiform discharges, no focal slowing, and no seizures captured during this 60-minute routine recording. Of note, incidental finding of occasional premature ventricular complexes on the ECG artifact channel. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Routine 60-minute recording. Sensitivity of a single interictal EEG for detecting epileptiform abnormalities is approximately 50%. Events were not captured during this study.

findings: Normal sinus rhythm at 72 bpm
Normal PR, QRS, and QTc intervals
Left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria (Sokolow-Lyon: SV1 + RV5 = 38 mm)
Occasional premature ventricular complexes (2 captured on tracing), with left bundle branch block morphology and inferior axis suggesting right ventricular outflow tract origin
No ST-segment elevation or depression
No Brugada pattern
No epsilon waves
T-wave inversions in leads V1-V3
ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm with voltage criteria for left ventricular hypertrophy. Occasional PVCs with LBBB morphology. T-wave inversions in V1-V3 may represent LVH-related repolarization abnormality, though anterior T-wave inversions in the setting of exertional syncope and possible RV origin PVCs should raise consideration for arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy or hypertrophic cardiomyopathy. The resting ECG does not capture a rhythm disturbance to explain the syncopal episodes.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: BNP 195 pg/mL (H), CK total 255 U/L (H), Magnesium 2.3 mg/dL (H).

findings: Interventricular septum thickness (diastole): 2.1
Posterior wall thickness: 1.2
Diastolic function: Grade II diastolic dysfunction (pseudonormal pattern)
Right ventricular function: Normal
Pericardial effusion: None
output.impression: Abnormal values: Interventricular septum thickness (diastole) 2.1 cm (H), Posterior wall thickness 1.2 cm (H), Left ventricular outflow tract gradient (Valsalva) 62 mmHg (H), Systolic anterior motion of mitral valve Present with Valsalva qualitative (H), Mitral regurgitation Moderate (with Valsalva provocation) qualitative (H), Left atrial diameter 4.1 cm (H), Diastolic function Grade II diastolic dysfunction (pseudonormal pattern) qualitative (H).

output.rhythm_summary: Predominantly normal sinus rhythm with multiple episodes of non-sustained ventricular tachycardia

events.description: Baseline normal sinus rhythm with average rate 74 bpm (range 56-118 bpm)
Seven episodes of non-sustained ventricular tachycardia (NSVT), longest run 11 beats at rate 185 bpm, occurring at 14:32 during reported brisk walking
Additional NSVT runs of 4-8 beats at rates 160-190 bpm
Frequent premature ventricular complexes: 1,847 total in 24 hours (approximately 2.1% burden), predominantly with LBBB morphology and inferior axis
Sinus tachycardia to 118 bpm during reported exercise with appropriate increase and recovery
No significant pauses (longest RR interval 1.4 seconds during sleep)
No atrial fibrillation or supraventricular tachycardia

findings: Baseline normal sinus rhythm with average rate 74 bpm (range 56-118 bpm)
Seven episodes of non-sustained ventricular tachycardia (NSVT), longest run 11 beats at rate 185 bpm, occurring at 14:32 during reported brisk walking
Additional NSVT runs of 4-8 beats at rates 160-190 bpm
Frequent premature ventricular complexes: 1,847 total in 24 hours (approximately 2.1% burden), predominantly with LBBB morphology and inferior axis
Three episodes of ventricular couplets
Sinus tachycardia to 118 bpm during reported exercise with appropriate increase and recovery
No significant pauses (longest RR interval 1.4 seconds during sleep)
No atrial fibrillation or supraventricular tachycardia
Heart rate variability slightly reduced
output.impression: 24-hour Holter monitoring reveals multiple episodes of non-sustained ventricular tachycardia (7 runs, longest 11 beats at 185 bpm) along with a moderately elevated PVC burden (2.1%). The NSVT occurred predominantly during periods of increased physical activity. In the context of suspected hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, NSVT is a major risk factor for sudden cardiac death and is an important component of the SCD risk stratification.

findings.region: Interventricular septum, predominantly basal and mid-ventricular segments
findings.signal: T1: Mildly elevated native T1 values in areas of maximal hypertrophy (1120 ms, normal <1050 ms); T2: Normal, no edema; cine: Preserved systolic function, LVEF 62%. Dynamic LVOT obstruction visualized with SAM of the mitral valve; late_gadolinium_enhancement: Patchy mid-wall late gadolinium enhancement at the RV insertion points and within the hypertrophied septum, representing approximately 12% of LV mass. Pattern consistent with myocardial fibrosis in HCM.

output.impression: Significant late gadolinium enhancement (12% of LV mass) indicates extensive myocardial fibrosis, which is an established substrate for ventricular arrhythmias and a recognized risk marker for sudden cardiac death in HCM. Dynamic LVOT obstruction with SAM is visualized. LVEF is preserved. The combination of severe hypertrophy (>30 mm approaches threshold), extensive LGE, exertional syncope, family history of SCD, and NSVT places this patient at high risk for sudden cardiac death. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings.description: Achieved 7.2 METs on modified Bruce protocol, test terminated at Stage III due to presyncope
Peak heart rate 148 bpm (85% of age-predicted maximum)
Resting BP 140/84 mmHg, peak exercise BP 118/72 mmHg (abnormal hypotensive response to exercise)
Abnormal blood pressure response: systolic BP failed to augment and dropped 22 mmHg from rest to peak exercise
One run of non-sustained ventricular tachycardia (8 beats at 192 bpm) at peak exercise, coinciding with onset of presyncope
No significant ST-segment changes to suggest ischemia
Frequent PVCs during exercise and early recovery (14 total)
Symptoms: lightheadedness and near-syncope at peak exercise prompting test termination

quantitative_data.rhythm: Sinus rhythm with provoked non-sustained ventricular tachycardia and significant LVOT gradient augmentation during exercise

output.impression: Exercise stress test reveals an abnormal hypotensive blood pressure response (systolic BP drop of 22 mmHg from rest to peak exercise), which in the context of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy reflects worsening dynamic LVOT obstruction with exercise. Provoked NSVT at peak exercise. The exercise-induced hemodynamic compromise reproduces the clinical scenario of the patient's exertional syncopal episodes, confirming the arrhythmic and obstructive mechanism.

additional_observations: No mass lesion or enhancing abnormality
output.impression: Brain MRI is entirely normal. No acute or chronic intracranial pathology identified. No structural lesion to suggest a seizure focus or other neurological cause for the patient's episodes of loss of consciousness. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.query: hypertrophic cardiomyopathy exertional syncope sudden cardiac death risk stratification

results.title: 2024 AHA/ACC Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy
results.key_finding: ICD recommended for HCM patients with >=1 major risk factor for SCD: family history of SCD, maximum wall thickness >=30mm, unexplained syncope, NSVT, abnormal BP response to exercise. Extensive LGE on CMR (>=15% of LV mass) is an emerging risk modifier.

results.title: Unexplained syncope in hypertrophic cardiomyopathy: prevalence, significance, and implications
results.key_finding: Unexplained syncope within 6 months of evaluation is a major risk factor for SCD in HCM. Exertional syncope carries higher risk than syncope at rest. The mechanism involves ventricular tachyarrhythmia, dynamic LVOT obstruction, or both.

results.title: Convulsive syncope mimicking epilepsy in hypertrophic cardiomyopathy
results.key_finding: Cardiac syncope with convulsive movements (myoclonic jerks from cerebral hypoperfusion) is commonly misdiagnosed as epileptic seizures. Key distinguishing features include brevity of convulsive movements (<15 seconds), exertional triggers, rapid recovery without post-ictal confusion, and normal EEG.

output.summary: Current evidence strongly supports ICD implantation for primary prevention of sudden cardiac death in HCM patients with multiple risk factors. This patient has at least three established risk factors: family history of SCD (father age 49), unexplained exertional syncope, and NSVT on Holter monitoring. The maximum wall thickness of 23 mm approaches the 30 mm threshold. Extensive LGE (12% of LV mass) is an additional risk modifier. The ESC HCM Risk-SCD calculator and AHA/ACC risk stratification algorithms both support ICD implantation in this clinical scenario.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Cardiac syncope due to hypertrophic cardiomyopathy with dynamic left ventricular outflow tract obstruction and non-sustained ventricular tachycardia

differential.key_distinguishing: Normal EEG, normal prolactin, no post-ictal confusion, very brief convulsive movements (<15 seconds) consistent with convulsive syncope rather than true seizure. No tongue bite or incontinence. Immediate return to baseline cognition. Exertional trigger atypical for epilepsy.

No prodrome of nausea, diaphoresis, or tunnel vision. No identifiable vasovagal trigger (pain, emotional stress, prolonged standing). Exclusively exertional onset argues strongly against vasovagal mechanism. Negative tilt table test.
VT is present but is secondary to the underlying HCM substrate rather than idiopathic. The structural heart disease (HCM) is the unifying diagnosis.

T-wave inversions in V1-V3 and LBBB-morphology PVCs could initially suggest ARVC, but echocardiogram and cardiac MRI show normal RV without dilation, wall motion abnormality, or fatty replacement. The pathology is clearly left-sided septal hypertrophy consistent with HCM.

QTc is normal (442 ms) on resting ECG. No family history suggestive of channelopathy. Morphology of the arrhythmia (monomorphic VT) is more consistent with structural heart disease than polymorphic VT/torsades de pointes seen in LQTS.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain 12-lead ECG on presentation to evaluate for arrhythmia or conduction abnormality
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal sinus rhythm with LVH by voltage criteria, PVCs, and T-wave inversions V1-V3 — no acute arrhythmia captured but features raise suspicion for structural heart disease

optimal_actions.action: Obtain comprehensive labs including CBC, BMP, troponin, BNP, TSH, lactate, and prolactin to evaluate metabolic and cardiac causes and assess for seizure biomarkers
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal metabolic panel, normal troponin, mildly elevated BNP, normal prolactin (argues against generalized seizure)

optimal_actions.action: Obtain routine EEG to evaluate for epileptiform abnormalities given referral diagnosis of seizures
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal EEG with no epileptiform discharges — does not support epilepsy diagnosis

optimal_actions.action: Critically re-evaluate the history: recognize that brief convulsive movements with exertional onset, no post-ictal confusion, and rapid recovery are characteristic of convulsive syncope rather than epileptic seizure
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Reframe the clinical picture from 'seizure' to 'cardiac syncope with convulsive features'

optimal_actions.action: Order transthoracic echocardiogram to evaluate for structural heart disease given LVH on ECG, exertional syncope, family history of sudden cardiac death, and systolic murmur increasing with Valsalva
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Severe asymmetric septal hypertrophy (IVS 2.1 cm), dynamic LVOT obstruction with Valsalva (gradient 62 mmHg), SAM of mitral valve — diagnosis of hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy

optimal_actions.action: Order 24-48 hour Holter monitor to assess for ventricular arrhythmias
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Multiple runs of NSVT (longest 11 beats at 185 bpm), frequent PVCs — major SCD risk factor in HCM

optimal_actions.action: Order cardiac MRI with gadolinium for tissue characterization and risk stratification
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Maximum wall thickness 23 mm, patchy mid-wall LGE representing 12% of LV mass — confirms HCM with fibrosis substrate for arrhythmia

optimal_actions.action: Urgent cardiology and electrophysiology referral for SCD risk stratification and ICD evaluation
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Multiple major risk factors identified: family history of SCD, exertional syncope, NSVT, extensive LGE

optimal_actions.action: Discontinue levetiracetam as the diagnosis is cardiac syncope, not epilepsy
Counsel patient to immediately cease competitive and vigorous athletic activities pending cardiology evaluation, per ACC/AHA guidelines for HCM
Arrange genetic counseling and first-degree family screening with ECG and echocardiography (brother with palpitations is especially concerning)

critical_actions: Recognize that the episodes are cardiac syncope with convulsive features, not epileptic seizures
Order echocardiogram to identify hypertrophic cardiomyopathy as the underlying cause
Order Holter monitoring to detect non-sustained ventricular tachycardia
Urgent cardiology referral for SCD risk stratification and ICD evaluation
Restrict the patient from competitive and vigorous athletics immediately
Screen family members, especially the brother with palpitations
contraindicated_actions: Continuing or escalating antiepileptic drug therapy for a misdiagnosed seizure disorder
Allowing the patient to return to competitive athletics or vigorous coaching activities before cardiac evaluation
Administering isoproterenol or dobutamine without cardiology supervision (could provoke fatal arrhythmia in HCM)
Prescribing vasodilators or high-dose diuretics that could worsen LVOT obstruction by reducing preload
Discharging the patient without echocardiography and cardiac monitoring given family history of sudden cardiac death and exertional syncope
key_reasoning_points: The referral diagnosis of 'new-onset seizures' is a classic diagnostic pitfall — convulsive syncope (brief myoclonic jerks from cerebral hypoperfusion) is frequently misdiagnosed as epilepsy, and up to 20% of patients referred to epilepsy clinics actually have cardiac syncope
The exclusively exertional trigger for loss of consciousness is a critical red flag for cardiac syncope, as epileptic seizures are rarely provoked specifically by vigorous exercise

The absence of post-ictal confusion and immediate return to normal cognition after each episode strongly favors syncope over seizure — true generalized tonic-clonic seizures are followed by minutes to hours of confusion
The normal prolactin level argues against a generalized tonic-clonic seizure, as prolactin typically rises 2-3 fold within 20 minutes of a generalized seizure

The family history of sudden cardiac death at a young age (father at 49), a relative with an ICD, and a brother with palpitations strongly suggests a familial cardiomyopathy, most likely HCM caused by a sarcomeric protein mutation

The systolic murmur that increases with Valsalva maneuver on cardiac exam is a classic finding of hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy and should have triggered immediate echocardiographic evaluation
The normal initial ECG is a major source of diagnostic difficulty in this case — the arrhythmia is paroxysmal and not captured at rest, and LVH voltage criteria alone are nonspecific
T-wave inversions in V1-V3 with LBBB-morphology PVCs could initially mislead toward ARVC, but the echocardiogram and cardiac MRI clearly demonstrate HCM
HCM is the most common cause of sudden cardiac death in young athletes, and this patient's role as a basketball coach participating in vigorous drills places him at significant risk during exertion

metadata.diagnostic_trap: Patient referred with label of 'new-onset seizures' due to witnessed convulsive movements during syncope; normal initial ECG reinforces the seizure misdiagnosis
clinical_pearls: Convulsive syncope occurs in up to 90% of induced syncope episodes and is frequently misdiagnosed as epileptic seizures
Exertional syncope in a patient under 60 with a family history of sudden death should immediately prompt evaluation for hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, ARVC, or channelopathies
A systolic murmur that increases with Valsalva or standing is nearly pathognomonic for LVOT obstruction in HCM — most other murmurs decrease with these maneuvers
Normal resting ECG does not exclude serious cardiac arrhythmia as the cause of syncope; ambulatory monitoring is essential
The combination of unexplained syncope, NSVT, family history of SCD, severe LVH, and extensive LGE on cardiac MRI confers high risk for sudden cardiac death in HCM and warrants ICD implantation
